title: Guides, policies and trainings on electronic federal records (Coffee County Sheriff's Office)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Alabama Open Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

- All guides, memos or briefings on storing and handling electronic records, including but not limited to text messages, as federal records. 

- All polices on storing and handling electronic records, including but not limited to text messages, as federal records. 

-  All employee training materials on storing and handling electronic records, including but not limited to text messages, as federal records.

- All communications records and their surrounding context containing references, to but not limited to, the following agencies and terms: ICE, FBI, HSI, ERO, IDF, 'Mobile Fortify', Flock, Ring, ALPR, drones, Pegasus, Graphite

Fee Waiver

This request concerns matters of significant public interest involving government surveillance and constitutional rights, and any fees should be waived or minimized.

I look forward to your response within the statutory timeframe.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

André Natta, a citizen of Alabama, in coordination with Jonathan Harris.

title: LONGVIEW SCHOOL DISTRICT – 2026 CONTRACT RENEWAL  Superintendent Karen Cloninger

communications.communication:
Via email: publicrecordsrequest@longviewschools.org

PUBLIC RECORDS REQUEST [RCW 42.56]

TO: Public Records Officer, Longview School District

DATE: June 1, 2026

Dear Public Records Officer,

Pursuant to the Washington State Public Records Act (RCW 42.56), I am requesting copies of the following identifiable public records and writings that were created, received, maintained, or relied upon in the care, custody, or control of the Longview School District between January 1, 2026, and the present.

1. Scope of Records Requested
Please produce complete, unredacted (except where strictly exempted by statute) electronic copies of the following:

A. Superintendent Employment Contracts:

The complete, fully executed employment contract, rolling extension, or contract renewal for Superintendent Dr. Karen Cloninger approved by the School Board between April 1, 2026, and May 31, 2026.

All previous iterations, addenda, and signature pages of Dr. Cloninger’s employment contracts active during the 2025–2026 school year.

B. Meeting Materials and Minutes:

All agendas, official minutes, meeting recordings (audio or video), transcription files, and hand-written or digital notes taken by board members or the board secretary during any regular, special, or emergency meetings of the Longview School Board held between April 1, 2026, and May 31, 2026.

All records, resolutions, or motions presented, discussed, or voted upon during these meetings relating to the retention, contract extension, or placement on administrative leave of Dr. Karen Cloninger.

C. Legal Fee Indemnification and Defense Records:

All records, resolutions, or board authorizations regarding the district's payment, advancement, or reimbursement of legal defense fees, defense counsel retainers, or any statutory indemnification (pursuant to RCW 28A.320.100 or district policy) for Dr. Karen Cloninger or Andrew Schoonover regarding ongoing criminal or administrative investigations.

All invoices, billing statements, fee approvals, or check registers reflecting payments made by the district to defense firms (including but not limited to Jon McMullen / McMullen Law Offices) from January 1, 2026, to the present.

D. Targeted Communications Regarding Contract and Leave:

All emails, text messages, and instant messages (such as Microsoft Teams) exchanged among Longview School Board members, the Board President, and Acting Superintendent Patti Bowen regarding the timing, emergency justification, public backlash, or scheduling of the contract renewal or subsequent emergency administrative leave for Dr. Cloninger. 

DEFINITION OF RECORDS AND WRITINGS

RECORDS AND WRITINGS as defined by RCW 42.56.010(4): any handwriting, typewriting, printing, photostating, photographing, and every other means of recording any form of communication or representation including, but not limited to, letters, words, pictures, sounds, or symbols, or combination thereof, and all papers, maps, magnetic or paper tapes, photographic films and prints, motion picture, film and video recordings, magnetic or punched cards, discs, drums, diskettes, sound recordings, and other documents including existing data compilations from which information may be obtained or translated.

PRODUCTION PROCESSING AND DELIVERY OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS

Please provide all records in their native electronic format. Please do not instead create new records by printing electronic files then scanning and printing the newly scanned prints.

To preserve metadata and ease review, please produce as follows:

Emails: PST ( Microsoft Outlook ) or MBOX containers; if unavailable, EML/MSG with full headers.
Chats/Texts/IM: native exports where supported; otherwise, CSV/JSON with fields for sender, recipients, timestamps (UTC), message/thread IDs, and attachments (export attachments in original formats).
Calendars: ICS where possible; or PDF plus any attachments.
Documents/attachments: original/native formats (DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, PDF, etc.).
Logs/Reports: CSV/JSON preferred.
Delivery: Direct electronic transmission via email attachments or open (no login/registration/password) download link hosted by your agency (e.g., SharePoint/OneDrive public link). Please do not use third-party portals that require creating an account or logging in. A single or series of .zip files is fine.

If any records cannot be produced in the above formats, please produce them in their native format with associated metadata intact.

METADATA REQUESTED

Where reasonably available, please include customary metadata fields such as: From, To, CC, BCC, Subject, Sent/Received Date-Time (with timezone/UTC), Message-ID, Conversation/Thread-ID, file names, file sizes, hash values (if held), and folder/location paths.

SEARCH DETAILS AND RECORDS PRESERVATION

Please identify the systems searched e.g.  (Exchange/Outlook/Office 365, Teams, mobile devices, MDM archives, JustFOIA/CivicPlus admin, helpdesk, file shares) and the custodians.

Please describe the search methods/terms used.

If any potentially responsive sources were not searched or are no longer available, please explain why and what preservation steps were taken.

USE OF THIRD PARTY PORTALS NOT MANDATORY

Please note that under Washington’s Public Records Act, use of a third-party “portal” is not mandatory for requestors. Agencies may not condition access to records on the creation of an account, the use of a login, or the acceptance of third-party vendor terms.

Third-party portals (such as GovQA, NextRequest, JustFOIA, or similar systems) often:

Require account creation or login credentials;

Restrict downloads, viewing times, or re-access;

Remove or disable links after a limited period;

Place records behind commercial platforms operated by for-profit corporations.

These restrictions unlawfully burden and condition access to public records. The Public Records Act forbids agencies from imposing access barriers beyond those authorized by statute.

RCW 42.56.080(2): An agency “shall not deny a request for identifiable public records solely on the basis that the request is overbroad.” Agencies must provide the “fullest assistance” and “most timely possible action” to requestors.

RCW 42.56.550(1): Courts are directed to liberally construe the PRA to ensure access and strictly construe exemptions against withholding.

Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014): Agencies must produce records in a “reasonably usable format when feasible.”

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure and cannot be restricted by agency practices.

Because of this, I respectfully decline use of any third-party portal. Please provide all responsive records via:

Direct electronic email attachment to this requestor’s address, or

A direct, open (no password, no registration required) cloud-based download link hosted by your agency.

Do not register this requestor with, nor direct this requestor to, any third-party commercial portal, including but not limited to FOIAOnline, GovQA (a Granicus Corporation), NextRequest, FOIAExpress, JustFOIA, FOIADirect, or any similar online service.

Do not transmit any portion of this request, or responsive records, to any for-profit vendor or portal. Doing so would unlawfully condition access to public records and contravene both RCW 42.56.080 and RCW 42.56.550.

PUBLIC RECORDS REQUESTS SUBMITTED VIA EMAIL

This public records request is being submitted via email, consistent with Washington’s Public Records Act.

RCW 42.56.080(2) provides in relevant part:

“Agencies shall honor requests received in person during an agency’s normal office hours, or by mail or email, for identifiable public records unless exempted by provisions of this chapter. No official format is required for making a records request; however, agencies may recommend that requestors submit requests using an agency-provided form or web page.”

Accordingly:

A request submitted by email is a valid, enforceable method of making a PRA request.

Agencies may recommend, but cannot require, the use of an online portal or official form.

Any refusal to honor this request as submitted would directly conflict with RCW 42.56.080 and the agency’s duty under RCW 42.56.100 to provide the “fullest assistance to requestors” and adopt reasonable rules that do not “unreasonably hinder or delay” access.

Therefore, this request is to be honored and fulfilled by your agency via email, as submitted. Should the agency fail to process this request in compliance with statute, relief will be sought pursuant to RCW 42.56.550 in the form of judicial review, penalties, and attorney’s fees.

INSTALLMENTS AND FEE ESTIMATES

Please provide:

An initial acknowledgment within five business days (RCW 42.56.520) with a reasonable time estimate for each installment, and

A cost estimate if fees are anticipated (please itemize and explain).

NISSEN AFFIDAVIT AND ALL DEVICE SEARCH REQUIRED

Pursuant to Nissen v. Pierce County, 183 Wn.2d 863 (2015), and its progeny, this request specifically includes all responsive public records contained on personal devices or personal accounts (including personal cell phones, text/SMS/MMS, personal email accounts, cloud storage, instant messaging apps, and social media accounts) of any agency employee, official, or contractor where public business may have been conducted.

To comply with this requirement, please:

Conduct a comprehensive all-device search, including both agency-issued and personally owned devices/accounts used for public business.

Require each potentially responsive custodian to execute a Nissen affidavit or sworn certification affirming:

That they conducted a diligent search of all personal and agency devices/accounts reasonably likely to contain responsive records;

The methods used to conduct the search (e.g., search terms, accounts checked, device types); and

Whether any responsive records were located, preserved, and produced.

Produce all responsive records located in such searches along with the executed affidavits.

Failure to obtain and provide sworn Nissen affidavits or equivalent certifications from custodians may constitute an incomplete search in violation of RCW 42.56.520 (requirement for a reasonable and adequate search) and may expose the agency to relief under RCW 42.56.550.

EXEMPTION LOGS

Please ensure that all redactions or exemptions claimed by your agency in the production of responsive records are accompanied by a complete and detailed exemption log.

The log must:

* Identify the valid legal reason (statutory citation) for each exemption;
* Specify the location of each redaction within the record produced (via footnote, coded reference, or margin notation);
* State the number of pages or records withheld in their entirety, if any; and
* Demonstrate that only the **minimal exempt portion** of any record has been withheld, as required by law.

The Washington Public Records Act requires agencies to justify each withholding or redaction:

RCW 42.56.210(3): “Agency responses refusing, in whole or in part, inspection of any public record shall include a statement of the specific exemption authorizing the withholding of the record (or part) and a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the record withheld.”
RCW 42.56.210(1): Exemptions must be narrowly construed.

Washington courts have repeatedly enforced this duty:

Rental Housing Ass’n of Puget Sound v. City of Des Moines, 165 Wn.2d 525, 538 (2009): Agencies must provide specific explanations for each exemption; conclusory or vague justifications are insufficient.
Sanders v. State*, 169 Wn.2d 827, 845 (2010): “The burden of proof is on the agency to establish that refusal to permit public inspection and copying is in accordance with a statute.”
Resident Action Council v. Seattle Housing Auth.* 177 Wn.2d 417, 433 (2013): Agencies must disclose the non-exempt portions*of records and cannot withhold more than necessary.

Therefore, I request that your agency provide an itemized exemption log with each installment of records produced, in compliance with RCW 42.56.210(3) and the above case law, ensuring transparency and accountability in the redaction process.

COMMERCIAL PURPOSE DECLARATION

Commercial Purpose Declaration

This public records request is not being made for any commercial purpose. The Washington State Public Records Act (PRA) prohibits agencies from disclosing “lists of individuals” for commercial purposes.

RCW 42.56.070(8) states:

“This chapter shall not be construed as giving authority to any agency to give, sell, or provide access to lists of individuals requested for commercial purposes….”

Accordingly:

This request does not seek a “list of individuals.”

This request is not made for any commercial purpose.

This declaration satisfies all requirements of RCW 42.56.070(8) regarding prohibitions on lists and commercial purposes.

Warning: The PRA does not authorize agencies to require requestors to complete additional “commercial purpose declarations” when the request does not seek a list of individuals. Such requirements improperly condition access to public records in violation of RCW 42.56.080(2) (requests must be honored by mail or email, no official format required) and RCW 42.56.100 (agencies must provide the fullest assistance to requestors and may not adopt rules that unreasonably hinder or delay access).

Washington courts have consistently rejected agency attempts to impose conditions not found in statute:

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): The PRA is a strongly worded mandate for broad disclosure; agencies cannot erect unauthorized barriers.

SEIU 925 v. University of Washington, 193 Wn.2d 860, 875 (2019): The PRA is to be liberally construed to promote access, and exemptions must be narrowly applied.

Therefore, no additional commercial purpose declaration will be completed for this request. Any attempt to impose such a requirement would be an unlawful restriction of rights under RCW 42.56.

PRESERVATION OF PUBLIC RECORDS *** ANTICIPATE LITIGATION ***

WAC 44-14-03005 RETENTION OF PUBLIC RECORDS

WAC 44-14-03005 (Retention of Public Records) makes clear that public records are subject to retention schedules, and that unlawful destruction can constitute a crime.

RCW 40.16.010 and RCW 40.16.020 criminalize the unlawful mutilation, destruction, or concealment of public records.

RCW 42.56.100 expressly prohibits an agency from destroying any public record after a public records request has been made for that record, even if the record would otherwise be scheduled for lawful destruction.

In addition, agencies have heightened retention obligations when litigation is reasonably anticipated. Records potentially relevant to actual or anticipated litigation must be preserved until both the public records request and the litigation have been fully resolved.

Legal Consequences of Premature Destruction

In Yacobellis v. City of Bellingham, 55 Wn. App. 706, 780 P.2d 272 (1989), the Court of Appeals held that an agency violates the Public Records Act and is subject to attorneys’ fees and statutory penalties when it destroys responsive records after receiving a public records request.

By contrast, it is not a PRA violation if a record was destroyed prior to the agency’s receipt of a request, provided the destruction was lawful under retention schedules. See Bldg. Indus. Ass’n of Wash. v. McCarthy, 152 Wn. App. 720, 218 P.3d 196 (2009); West v. Dep’t of Natural Resources, 163 Wn. App. 238, 258 P.3d 78 (2011).

Washington courts have emphasized that the Public Records Act (RCW 42.56) and the state’s records retention laws (RCW 40.14) are separate but complementary. Agencies must comply with both.

Accordingly:

Your agency must preserve all potentially responsive records immediately upon receipt of this request.

No responsive records may be altered, deleted, or destroyed until this request has been fully resolved and any related litigation has been lifted.

Any unlawful destruction of responsive records may expose the agency and its officials to criminal liability (RCW 40.16), civil penalties, and mandatory attorneys’ fees (RCW 42.56.550(4)).

RECORDS INSTALLMENTS - ORDER OF PRODUCTION

If production of responsive records will be completed in multiple installments, production in reverse chronological order is strongly preferred to the extent supported by native system exports, provided it does not delay the delivery of installments.

DIGITAL COMPRESSION AND FILE MINIMIZATION REQUESTED IN ACCORDANCE WITH RCW 42.56

Pursuant to RCW 42.56.080(2), please provide all responsive electronic records in the fewest number of individual files, preserving native formats wherever possible. Where appropriate, please use free digital compression (.zip or equivalent) to consolidate responsive records into the smallest number of installments for efficiency and economy.

This approach reduces agency burden, promotes prompt disclosure, and minimizes potential costs to the requestor, consistent with the Public Records Act’s directive that agencies provide records in the most “reasonably locatable form” and “fullest assistance to requestors.” See RCW 42.56.100 (agency duty to adopt reasonable rules for providing fullest assistance); RCW 42.56.120(2)(b) (fees must reflect actual costs, not used to discourage access).

Washington courts have consistently held that agencies must facilitate access in a practical, non-burdensome manner. See Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014) (agencies must provide records in a usable format when reasonably feasible); Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009) (PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure). Producing consolidated, digitally compressed files satisfies these obligations by reducing unnecessary fragmentation and excess per-page charges.

Accordingly, I request that each installment be produced as a single compressed .zip file (or as few as technically necessary), containing all responsive records in native format, to ensure both compliance with RCW 42.56 and an economical calculation of any applicable public records fees.

COMMUNICATION VIA EMAIL

Please send all requests for clarification, status updates, and production of responsive electronic documents via direct email reply.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. We look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Good Luck.

title: ALPR audit

communications.subject: The Missouri Sunshine Law Request: ALPR audit
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the The Missouri Sunshine Law, I hereby request the following records:

Concerning Flock Safety, these files;

1. Organization Audit in Flock.
The report should include data logged from the period of January 1, 2025, to the date this request is processed. Per Flock's documentation, the Organization Audit is available within the Insights tab and is defined as searches done within the agency.

2. Network Audit in Flock.
The report should include data logged from the period of January 1, 2025, to the date this request is processed. Per Flock's documentation, the Network Audit is available within the Insights tab and is defined as searches of the organization’s Flock network by any agency in the Flock System.

Typically the output for these audits is available in monthly batches.

3. A list of network share settings that include:
3a. Flock networks shared with me
3b. Networks that I’m sharing

Please provide records in their original electronic, machine readable format. For example, please provide csv or spreadsheet files where applicable.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 3 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Adam Hollis

title: LONGVIEW SCHOOL DISTRICT – Felony Arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger  and Mark Morris High School Sexual Assault Investigation.

communications.subject: Washington Public Records Act Request: LONGVIEW SCHOOL DISTRICT – Felony Arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger  and Mark Morris High School Sexual Assault Investigation.
communications.communication:
Via email: publicrecordsrequest@longviewschools.org

LONGVIEW SCHOOL DISTRICT – Felony Arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger  and Mark Morris High School Sexual Assault Investigation.
CC: WASHINGTON OFFICE OF SUPERINTENDENT OF PUBLIC INSTRUCTION
CC: SPOKANE PUBLIC SCHOOLS DISTRICT

Oregon Public Broadcasting: Longview superintendent arrested on obstruction charges for high school sexual assault investigation
https://www.opb.org/article/2026/05/21/longview-high-school-superintendent-arrested-on-obstruction-charges-for-sexual-assault-investigation/

KGW8: Court documents detail why Longview School District's superintendent is charged in an alleged cover-up of abuse
https://www.kgw.com/article/news/education/longview-school-district-superintdent-karen-cloninger-arrest-sex-assault-court-docs/283-a1d5d93e-ad17-4ded-87cf-e956448e3d7b

KOIN: ‘Don’t talk about this’: Longview school superintendent allegedly told staff not to report abuse
https://www.koin.com/news/washington/dont-talk-about-this-longview-school-superintendent-allegedly-told-staff-not-to-report-abuse/

To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Washington Public Records Act [RCW 42.56] I hereby request the following records:

1. A complete copy of any and all records and writings regarding the investigation and subsequent arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger.
2. The complete .pst file(s) of any and all email accounts assigned to School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger in your agency possession.
3. The complete personnel file of School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger. 

Responsive records include but are not limited to:

Law Enforcement reports and communications
Parent and Student communications and notifications
Incident reports, incident analysis, emails, incident follow up
Media communications, press releases
Court records, witness statements, etc. 
All insurance records 
Copies of Investigative Reports
Legal records, legal emails, demand letters, etc.
Complaints and communications referencing assault
Copies of all public records requests for records concerning or relating to sexual assault at Morris High School and/or Superintendent Karen Cloninger.
All Communications of Superintendent Karen Cloninger January 1, 2024 - Present.
Email (including headers, message bodies, inline images, and attachments).
Text/SMS/MMS (including iMessage, Android, carrier SMS).
Instant messaging & collaboration: Microsoft Teams/Skype, Slack, Google Chat, Zoom/Zoom Chat, Webex, RingCentral, WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram, etc.
Calendars/Invites (meeting requests, agendas, minutes, chat during meetings).
Voicemail logs/transcripts/recordings and call logs (if used for vendor contact).
File shares & cloud drives that contain such communications (SharePoint/OneDrive/Google Drive/Box/Dropbox).
Letters, memos, meeting notes, handouts, and presentations
Related records about those communications
Contracts/agreements, SOWs, amendments, quotes, order forms, renewals, and NDAs.
Invoices, purchase orders, payment records, and W-9s.
Litigation hold(s) or preservation notices relating to these communications.

DEFINITION OF RECORDS AND WRITINGS

RECORDS AND WRITINGS as defined by RCW 42.56.010(4): any handwriting, typewriting, printing, photostating, photographing, and every other means of recording any form of communication or representation including, but not limited to, letters, words, pictures, sounds, or symbols, or combination thereof, and all papers, maps, magnetic or paper tapes, photographic films and prints, motion picture, film and video recordings, magnetic or punched cards, discs, drums, diskettes, sound recordings, and other documents including existing data compilations from which information may be obtained or translated.

PRODUCTION PROCESSING AND DELIVERY OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS

Please provide all records in their native electronic format. Please do not instead create new records by printing electronic files then scanning and printing the newly scanned prints.

To preserve metadata and ease review, please produce as follows:

Emails: PST ( Microsoft Outlook ) or MBOX containers; if unavailable, EML/MSG with full headers.
Chats/Texts/IM: native exports where supported; otherwise, CSV/JSON with fields for sender, recipients, timestamps (UTC), message/thread IDs, and attachments (export attachments in original formats).
Calendars: ICS where possible; or PDF plus any attachments.
Documents/attachments: original/native formats (DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, PDF, etc.).
Logs/Reports: CSV/JSON preferred.
Delivery: Direct electronic transmission via email attachments or open (no login/registration/password) download link hosted by your agency (e.g., SharePoint/OneDrive public link). Please do not use third-party portals that require creating an account or logging in. A single or series of .zip files is fine.

If any records cannot be produced in the above formats, please produce them in their native format with associated metadata intact.

METADATA REQUESTED

Where reasonably available, please include customary metadata fields such as: From, To, CC, BCC, Subject, Sent/Received Date-Time (with timezone/UTC), Message-ID, Conversation/Thread-ID, file names, file sizes, hash values (if held), and folder/location paths.

SEARCH DETAILS AND RECORDS PRESERVATION

Please identify the systems searched e.g.  (Exchange/Outlook/Office 365, Teams, mobile devices, MDM archives, JustFOIA/CivicPlus admin, helpdesk, file shares) and the custodians.

Please describe the search methods/terms used.

If any potentially responsive sources were not searched or are no longer available, please explain why and what preservation steps were taken.

USE OF THIRD PARTY PORTALS NOT MANDATORY

Please note that under Washington’s Public Records Act, use of a third-party “portal” is not mandatory for requestors. Agencies may not condition access to records on the creation of an account, the use of a login, or the acceptance of third-party vendor terms.

Third-party portals (such as GovQA, NextRequest, JustFOIA, or similar systems) often:

Require account creation or login credentials;

Restrict downloads, viewing times, or re-access;

Remove or disable links after a limited period;

Place records behind commercial platforms operated by for-profit corporations.

These restrictions unlawfully burden and condition access to public records. The Public Records Act forbids agencies from imposing access barriers beyond those authorized by statute.

RCW 42.56.080(2): An agency “shall not deny a request for identifiable public records solely on the basis that the request is overbroad.” Agencies must provide the “fullest assistance” and “most timely possible action” to requestors.

RCW 42.56.550(1): Courts are directed to liberally construe the PRA to ensure access and strictly construe exemptions against withholding.

Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014): Agencies must produce records in a “reasonably usable format when feasible.”

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure and cannot be restricted by agency practices.

Because of this, I respectfully decline use of any third-party portal. Please provide all responsive records via:

Direct electronic email attachment to this requestor’s address, or

A direct, open (no password, no registration required) cloud-based download link hosted by your agency.

Do not register this requestor with, nor direct this requestor to, any third-party commercial portal, including but not limited to FOIAOnline, GovQA (a Granicus Corporation), NextRequest, FOIAExpress, JustFOIA, FOIADirect, or any similar online service.

Accordingly:

Any refusal to honor this request as submitted would directly conflict with RCW 42.56.080 and the agency’s duty under RCW 42.56.100 to provide the “fullest assistance to requestors” and adopt reasonable rules that do not “unreasonably hinder or delay” access.

Therefore, this request is to be honored and fulfilled by your agency via email, as submitted. Should the agency fail to process this request in compliance with statute, relief will be sought pursuant to RCW 42.56.550 in the form of judicial review, penalties, and attorney’s fees.

INSTALLMENTS AND FEE ESTIMATES

Please provide:

An initial acknowledgment within five business days (RCW 42.56.520) with a reasonable time estimate for each installment, and

A cost estimate if fees are anticipated (please itemize and explain).

Pursuant to Nissen v. Pierce County, 183 Wn.2d 863 (2015), and its progeny, this request specifically includes all responsive public records contained on personal devices or personal accounts (including personal cell phones, text/SMS/MMS, personal email accounts, cloud storage, instant messaging apps, and social media accounts) of any agency employee, official, or contractor where public business may have been conducted.

To comply with this requirement, please:

Conduct a comprehensive all-device search, including both agency-issued and personally owned devices/accounts used for public business.

Require each potentially responsive custodian to execute a Nissen affidavit or sworn certification affirming:

That they conducted a diligent search of all personal and agency devices/accounts reasonably likely to contain responsive records;

The methods used to conduct the search (e.g., search terms, accounts checked, device types); and

Whether any responsive records were located, preserved, and produced.

Produce all responsive records located in such searches along with the executed affidavits.

Failure to obtain and provide sworn Nissen affidavits or equivalent certifications from custodians may constitute an incomplete search in violation of RCW 42.56.520 (requirement for a reasonable and adequate search) and may expose the agency to relief under RCW 42.56.550.

Please ensure that all redactions or exemptions claimed by your agency in the production of responsive records are accompanied by a complete and detailed exemption log.

* Identify the valid legal reason (statutory citation) for each exemption;
* Specify the location of each redaction within the record produced (via footnote, coded reference, or margin notation);
* State the number of pages or records withheld in their entirety, if any; and
* Demonstrate that only the **minimal exempt portion** of any record has been withheld, as required by law.

The Washington Public Records Act requires agencies to justify each withholding or redaction:

RCW 42.56.210(3): “Agency responses refusing, in whole or in part, inspection of any public record shall include a statement of the specific exemption authorizing the withholding of the record (or part) and a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the record withheld.”

Washington courts have repeatedly enforced this duty:

Rental Housing Ass’n of Puget Sound v. City of Des Moines, 165 Wn.2d 525, 538 (2009): Agencies must provide specific explanations for each exemption; conclusory or vague justifications are insufficient.

Resident Action Council v. Seattle Housing Auth.* 177 Wn.2d 417, 433 (2013): Agencies must disclose the non-exempt portions*of records and cannot withhold more than necessary.

Therefore, I request that your agency provide an itemized exemption log with each installment of records produced, in compliance with RCW 42.56.210(3) and the above case law, ensuring transparency and accountability in the redaction process.

COMMERCIAL PURPOSE DECLARATION

Commercial Purpose Declaration

This public records request is not being made for any commercial purpose. The Washington State Public Records Act (PRA) prohibits agencies from disclosing “lists of individuals” for commercial purposes.

RCW 42.56.070(8) states:

“This chapter shall not be construed as giving authority to any agency to give, sell, or provide access to lists of individuals requested for commercial purposes….”

Accordingly:

This request does not seek a “list of individuals.”

This request is not made for any commercial purpose.

This declaration satisfies all requirements of RCW 42.56.070(8) regarding prohibitions on lists and commercial purposes.

Warning: The PRA does not authorize agencies to require requestors to complete additional “commercial purpose declarations” when the request does not seek a list of individuals. Washington courts have consistently rejected agency attempts to impose conditions not found in statute:

WAC 44-14-03005 (Retention of Public Records) makes clear that public records are subject to retention schedules, and that unlawful destruction can constitute a crime.

App. 830, 841 (2009) (PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure). The requested documents will be made available to the general public.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. We look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Good Luck.

title: Accurint / AVCC Contracting and Integration Documents

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Oklahoma Open Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

On April 21st, Chief Bacy stated that an addendum was put in place with Accurint to codify the pre-existing agreement that Accurint cannot use OKCPD's data as their own and share it. We are requesting the following:

1) The addendum that was put in place between OKCPD and LexisNexis/Accurint
2) The pre-existing contract, agreement, or other documents that cover the "pre-existing agreement with Accurint" mentioned. We already have the Schedule A documents attached to previous meeting agendas (https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1LkviqZ2mMpFSWG8ZCv-b5kOMq6Vq29w6?usp=drive_link), and are looking for any additional agreements between your agency that outline how data is shared with Accurint/PSDEX/LexisNexis. 
3) We are seeking documents that describe what OKCPD data is shared with consortium members (suspect info, address info, victim info, info on minors, witness details, 911 caller info, etc.). An API/XML or other type of connection is required between OKCPD systems and LexisNexis to facilitate this sharing. We are trying to understand what data fields are shared. To help answer this question, please provide one or more:
  - A) Any Interface Control Document (ICD) or technical implementation guide that defines the data structure and data elements for the automated export of Records Management System (RMS) or Computer-Aided Dispatch (CAD) data to Accurint/PSDEX/Consortium Data Warehouse.
  - B) Any "Data Translation" guides, field-mapping tables, or schemas used to configure the transfer of local agency data into the LexisNexis Public Safety Data Exchange (PSDEX) or Accurint Virtual Crime Center (VCC) platforms.
  - C) Field Lists: Any record defining the "Database Interface" capabilities, specifically identifying which categories of information (e.g., Victim PII, Witness Contact Data, Real-Time Jail Bookings) are included in consortium sharing arrangement.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

Shawn Segal

communications.subject: Spokane Public Schools - Public Records Request PRR-2026-147: Confirmation
communications.communication:
Dear Requester, 
   
 Spokane Public Schools (District 81) has received your request for records.  
   
   
 The following contains your tracking number and security key for your request 
  Request Number: PRR-2026-147 
  D45E0C8E. 
   
   
  
 If your request is a Public Records Request, your request will be forwarded to the relevant department(s) to determine the location, amount, and nature of our records, if any, that may be responsive to your request in accordance with Washington state's Public Records Act (RCW 42.56). We will respond to your initial request within 5 business days. 
  
 
If your request is for Student Records, student records requests and education verifications are processed in the order they are received and typically take 10–14 business days to complete. However, some requests may require additional time. In accordance with FERPA, requests for student records will be completed within 45 calendar days. 
  
 Please note that student records dated prior to 2000 are stored on microfilm, which has a manual search process. As a result, locating these records may take longer. 
  
   
  
 We are committed to fulfilling your request as quickly as possible and appreciate your patience. If you are receiving this email, rest assured, the District has received your request. Please let us know if you have any questions.

title: King County Directors Association (KCDA) - Comprehensive Time, Accountability, Agency Resource Utilization, and Compensation Records (October 1, 2025 – June 1, 2026)

communications.subject: Washington Public Records Act Request: King County Directors Association (KCDA) - Comprehensive Time, Accountability, Agency Resource Utilization, and Compensation Records (October 1, 2025 – June 1, 2026)
communications.communication:
TO: Public Records Officer
AGENCY: King County Directors Association (KCDA)
DATE: June 2, 2026

RE: Public Records Act Request – Comprehensive Time, Accountability, Agency Resource Utilization, and Compensation Records (October 1, 2025 – June 1, 2026)

Pursuant to the Washington State Public Records Act (RCW 42.56), I hereby request the disclosure and production of COPIES of the following public records:

To prevent any narrow construction, parsing, or omission of responsive records, the term "Personnel" as used in this request is defined globally and inclusively. It encompasses all individuals who performed work, held titles, or exercised authority on behalf of KCDA during the specified timeframe, regardless of classification. This explicitly includes, but is not limited to: all executive leadership, the Board of Directors, directors, department heads, managers, assistant managers, supervisors, HR personnel, Public Records Officers, coordinators, full-time employees, part-time employees, project-based staff, salaried/exempt staff, hourly/non-exempt staff, and any third-party temporary, leased, or contract staff acting in a supervisory, administrative, or operational capacity.

Scope of Request

The chronological scope of this request covers all records created, sent, received, or modified between October 1, 2025, and June 1, 2026, inclusive.

Categories of Records Requested

1. Comprehensive Timekeeping, Attendance, and Accountability Logs

 All raw timecard punch data, biometric/facial recognition scan logs, system event logs, and associated metadata generated by or stored within AMG Time (AMGtime), Attendance on Demand (AOD), or any other time-tracking software used by KCDA.

 All records of manual timecard adjustments, "mispunch" correction forms, supervisor approval overrides, edit histories, and system audit trails detailing who modified any time record, when the modification occurred, and the justification provided.

 All records of leave requests, paid time off (PTO), sick leave, compensatory time, vacation, or unauthorized absences submitted, approved, or denied within HRnext or any other human capital management database.

2. Payroll, Compensation, and Retirement Reporting Ledgers

 All itemized earnings statements, payroll ledger entries, labor distribution reports, and general ledger sheets generated through Payroll Solutions (Execupay) or internal accounting systems, showing the exact hours compensated, wage rates, and the specific public budget codes or cost centers utilized.

 All monthly retirement transmittal reports, service credit logs, and hour verification data submitted to the Washington State Department of Retirement Systems (DRS) for the Public Employees' Retirement System (PERS) or Deferred Compensation Program (DCP).

3. Digital Footprints, Calendar Metrics, and Communication Logs

 Complete, unredacted calendar data (including Microsoft Outlook, Google Calendar, or other digital schedules) for all Personnel, including all metadata, meeting descriptions, location fields, attendee lists, and invitations.
 
 All video conferencing account activity history logs (including but not limited to Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and Webex) associated with KCDA user credentials or public agency hardware, showing meeting dates, join/leave timestamps, IP addresses, and meeting IDs.
 
 System activity logs, audit trails, and metadata from Laserfiche, Google Drive, or internal network servers indicating every instance where digital files were accessed, viewed, edited, uploaded, or checked in/out by Personnel during standard business hours.

 Complete internal and external communication histories—including metadata logs from Proofpoint Essentials, email message bodies, attachments, and text/SMS messages on agency-issued devices—referencing, containing, or discussing any of the following terms or phrases: "Attorney General", "Model Rules", "testimony", "public comment", "hearing", "Zoom", "Kristen Deskin", "lawsuit", "or "Deskin".

4. Agency Representation, Travel, and Expenses

All travel vouchers, mileage logs, lodging receipts, meal reimbursements, and agency credit card (P-Card) statements submitted by or on behalf of any Personnel.
 
All internal authorizations, board resolutions, meeting minutes, or directives designating any individual to represent KCDA, deliver testimony, or submit formal commentary to any state agency, rule-making body, or professional association.

5. Production Processing and Delivery of Electronic Records

Please provide all records in their native electronic format to preserve metadata and ease review.

Logs and Data: Please produce all system, gateway, and connection log files in native tabular formats (such as CSV, XLSX, or JSON) rather than flattened PDF images.

Delivery: Direct electronic transmission via email attachment or an open, un-passworded download link hosted directly by your agency (e.g., a public SharePoint or OneDrive link) is requested. I respectfully decline the use of any restrictive third-party commercial portals that require account creation, login credentials, or acceptance of separate terms of service.

6. Search Details and Metadata

Please identify the specific systems searched (e.g., Proofpoint admin console, Exchange/Office 365 protection logs, IT firewall dashboards) and describe the search parameters or filtering terms used to isolate the responsive 550 error logs. If any log history within this timeframe has been purged, please state the standard retention schedule governing those specific files.

7. Installments and Fee Estimates
If the responsive records are voluminous, please provide them in installments as they become available. Please provide an initial acknowledgment within five business days as required under RCW 42.56.520, along with a reasonable time estimate for your first response or installment.

8. Digital Compression and File Minimization Requested in Accordance With RCW 42.56

Pursuant to RCW 42.56.080(2), please provide all responsive electronic records in the fewest number of individual files, preserving native formats wherever possible. Where appropriate, please use free digital compression (.zip or equivalent) to consolidate responsive records into the smallest number of installments for efficiency and economy.

This approach reduces agency burden, promotes prompt disclosure, and minimizes potential costs to the requestor, consistent with the Public Records Act’s directive that agencies provide records in the most “reasonably locatable form” and “fullest assistance to requestors.” See RCW 42.56.100 (agency duty to adopt reasonable rules for providing fullest assistance); RCW 42.56.120(2)(b) (fees must reflect actual costs, not used to discourage access).

Washington courts have consistently held that agencies must facilitate access in a practical, non-burdensome manner. See Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014) (agencies must provide records in a usable format when reasonably feasible); Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009) (PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure). Producing consolidated, digitally compressed files satisfies these obligations by reducing unnecessary fragmentation and excess per-page charges.

Accordingly, I request that each installment be produced as a single compressed .zip file (or as few as technically necessary), containing all responsive records in native format, to ensure both compliance with RCW 42.56 and an economical calculation of any applicable public records fees.

9. Exemption Logs

If KCDA claims any exemptions or applies redactions to any portion of the requested records, you are required under RCW 42.56.210(3) to provide a detailed exemption log identifying the specific statutory exemption and providing a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the withheld text or record.

This request is not commercial in nature and does not seek a list of any kind in any or in any form.

This request is not for profit.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: FOIA Request — Ukraine Energy Infrastructure Aid; Program Documents, Procurement Oversight, and Corruption Risk Assessments
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I hereby request copies of all records in the possession of the Department of State related to U.S. government energy infrastructure assistance to Ukraine in connection with the Russia-Ukraine war, including program documents, procurement oversight records, and any assessments of corruption or accountability risks in the delivery of energy aid.

RECORDS SOUGHT

I request all records from February 1, 2022 through December 31, 2024, including but not limited to:

Program strategy documents, cables, or internal guidance from the Bureau of European and Eurasian Affairs (EUR) or the Office of the Coordinator for U.S. Assistance to Europe and Eurasia governing energy infrastructure aid to Ukraine, including the provision of transformers, mobile heating units, boiler houses, and grid repair equipment;
Communications between State Department officials and USAID, the Department of Energy, or the National Security Council regarding the prioritization, design, or oversight of energy infrastructure assistance to Ukraine;
Records relating to the State Department's coordination with European partners — including the EU, EBRD, World Bank, or bilateral donors — on energy aid delivery to Ukraine;
Any diplomatic cables or internal assessments addressing corruption risks, aid diversion, or accountability concerns related to energy infrastructure assistance in Ukraine;
Records related to the State Department's interactions with Ukrenergo, DTEK, Naftogaz, or other Ukrainian state energy companies in connection with U.S.-funded energy assistance programs;
Any Inspector General or internal review records related to the oversight of energy sector assistance to Ukraine, to the extent not classified.

KEY OFFICIALS

Relevant officials likely include: Ambassador Amos Hochstein (Special Presidential Coordinator for Global Infrastructure and Energy Security), Assistant Secretary for European and Eurasian Affairs Karen Donfried, and the U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine. I ask that records custodians search email accounts and relevant shared drives for these officials for the requested period.

FEE WAIVER REQUEST

I request a waiver of all fees pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii). I am a journalist engaged in research for a book on U.S. energy policy and the geopolitical dimensions of the Russia-Ukraine war. The requested records concern how U.S. foreign assistance was directed toward Ukraine's energy sector and what accountability mechanisms were in place. Disclosure is in the public interest and will contribute significantly to public understanding of U.S. foreign assistance and energy policy. This request is not made for commercial purposes. If a full waiver is not granted, I request that fees be limited to duplication costs only, and that I be notified before any fees exceeding $25.00 are incurred.

EXPEDITED PROCESSING REQUEST

I request expedited processing pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E) on the grounds of urgency to inform the public concerning actual or alleged federal government activity related to U.S. foreign assistance and energy policy in Ukraine.

FORM OF PRODUCTION

Please produce all responsive records in electronic format (PDF or searchable text) via email or electronic delivery. If any records are withheld in whole or in part, I request a Vaughn index identifying each withheld record and the exemption(s) claimed. I also request that all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions of withheld records be released.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Tim McDonnell

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I hereby request copies of all records in the possession of the Department of State related to the negotiation and finalization of the United States–European Commission joint statement on energy security of March 25, 2022, in which the United States committed to strive to supply an additional 15 billion cubic meters (bcm) of liquefied natural gas (LNG) to the European Union in 2022, with increased volumes in subsequent years.

RECORDS SOUGHT

I request all records from January 1, 2022 through December 31, 2022, including but not limited to:

Preparatory materials, draft texts, talking points, and internal assessments produced prior to or during negotiations with the European Commission regarding the March 25, 2022 joint statement;
Communications between State Department officials and officials of the European Commission, EU member state governments, or other foreign governments related to the joint statement or U.S. LNG supply commitments;
Any analysis or modeling of U.S. capacity to meet the 15 bcm delivery commitment, including assessments of available export infrastructure, production capacity, or market constraints;
Communications between State Department officials and U.S. LNG producers, exporters, traders, or industry representatives — including but not limited to Cheniere Energy, Venture Global LNG, Freeport LNG, Sabine Pass Liquefaction, or their representatives — regarding the joint statement or related LNG supply to Europe;
Records reflecting any commitments, understandings, assurances, or representations made to specific companies regarding export volumes, regulatory treatment, or expedited authorizations in connection with the joint statement;
Communications between the Bureau of Energy Resources (ENR), the Office of the Special Presidential Coordinator for Global Infrastructure and Energy Security (Amos Hochstein), and the Office of the Secretary (Antony Blinken) related to the joint statement or its implementation;
Any interagency communications between the State Department and the Department of Energy, National Security Council, or National Economic Council regarding the joint statement or related LNG supply arrangements.

KEY OFFICIALS

To assist in your search, relevant officials likely include: Ambassador Amos Hochstein (Special Presidential Coordinator), Secretary Antony Blinken, and officials in the Bureau of Energy Resources (ENR). I ask that records custodians search email accounts, calendars, and relevant shared drives associated with these officials for the requested period.

FEE WAIVER REQUEST

I request a waiver of all fees associated with this request pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii). I am a journalist engaged in research for a book on U.S. energy policy and the geopolitical dimensions of the Russia-Ukraine war. Disclosure of the requested records is in the public interest as it is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of how the U.S. government coordinated its energy diplomacy response to the invasion of Ukraine, and how government-to-industry coordination shaped that response. This request is not made for commercial purposes. If a full waiver is not granted, I request that fees be limited to duplication costs only, and that I be notified before any fees exceeding $25.00 are incurred.

EXPEDITED PROCESSING REQUEST

I request expedited processing pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E) on the grounds that there is an urgency to inform the public concerning actual or alleged federal government activity. The U.S.-EU LNG agreement has had lasting consequences for European energy markets, U.S. domestic gas prices, and the long-term LNG export infrastructure buildout. Timely disclosure will directly contribute to ongoing public debate about U.S. energy export policy.

FORM OF PRODUCTION

Please produce all responsive records in electronic format (PDF or searchable text) via email or electronic delivery.

If any records are withheld in whole or in part, I request that you provide a Vaughn index identifying each withheld record, the exemption(s) claimed, and a particularized explanation of how the exemption applies. The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Tim McDonnell

title: Ukrainian Long-Range Drone Strikes on Russian Oil Infrastructure: Diplomatic Communications with European Allies; U.S. Policy Deliberations (January 1, 2024 – December 31, 2025)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I hereby request copies of all records in the possession of the Department of State related to Ukraine's campaign of long-range drone strikes against Russian oil refining and export infrastructure, and specifically: (1) diplomatic communications between the United States and European allies regarding concerns that such strikes could drive up global oil prices; (2) internal U.S. policy deliberations about whether to discourage or support Ukraine's oil infrastructure strike campaign; and (3) communications with Ukraine regarding the same. The requested time period is January 1, 2024 through December 31, 2025.

BACKGROUND

Beginning in late 2023 and intensifying in 2024, Ukraine conducted a sustained campaign of long-range drone strikes against Russian oil refineries, fuel depots, and export terminals, targeting facilities operated by Rosneft, Lukoil, Surgutneftegas, and others. These strikes generated significant public tension between the United States and Ukraine, with the Biden administration reportedly discouraging the campaign due to concerns about the effect on global oil prices. Several European governments — particularly those dependent on refined petroleum products from Russia or sensitive to fuel price inflation — also expressed concern. The diplomatic communications underlying these public tensions are the subject of this request.

RECORDS SOUGHT

All diplomatic cables, telegrams, or electronic messages between the State Department and U.S. embassies or European partner governments — including but not limited to Germany, France, Italy, Poland, Hungary, Slovakia, and the European Commission — related to Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil and energy infrastructure, including any communications reflecting European concerns about oil price or supply impacts;
Internal State Department memoranda, options papers, or policy assessments addressing U.S. policy toward Ukraine's oil infrastructure strike campaign, including any deliberations over whether to formally or informally discourage Ukraine from continuing those strikes;
Communications between State Department officials — including Secretary Antony Blinken, the Bureau of European and Eurasian Affairs, and the Office of the Special Presidential Coordinator for Global Infrastructure and Energy Security (Ambassador Amos Hochstein) — and Ukrainian officials regarding the oil infrastructure strike campaign;
Any readouts, memoranda of conversation, or summaries of meetings between U.S. officials and European counterparts in which Ukrainian strikes on Russian energy infrastructure were discussed;
Communications between the State Department and the National Security Council, National Economic Council, or Department of Energy regarding the diplomatic implications of Ukrainian strikes on Russian energy infrastructure;
Any State Department assessments of the impact of the oil infrastructure strike campaign on U.S. diplomatic relationships with European partners or on the cohesion of the Western coalition supporting Ukraine;
Records related to any U.S. representations to the Ukrainian government — formal or informal — requesting restraint or modification of the oil infrastructure strike campaign.

NOTE ON CABLE TRAFFIC

Diplomatic cables responsive to this request may be withheld in part under Exemption 1 (classified) or Exemption 5 (deliberative process). I ask that the Department apply these exemptions narrowly and produce all reasonably segregable, unclassified portions. In particular, cables that reflect the positions of foreign governments on oil price or energy market concerns — as distinct from operational or intelligence content — are likely to be releasable in substantial part and should be produced accordingly.

KEY OFFICIALS

Relevant officials include: Secretary Antony Blinken and Secretary Marco Rubio, Ambassador Amos Hochstein, the Assistant Secretary for European and Eurasian Affairs, and the U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine. I ask that records custodians search email accounts, relevant cable systems, and shared drives associated with these officials for the requested period.
FEE WAIVER REQUEST

I request a waiver of all fees pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii). I am a journalist engaged in research for a book on U.S. energy policy and the geopolitical dimensions of the Russia-Ukraine war. The diplomatic tensions between the United States, Ukraine, and European allies over the oil infrastructure strike campaign are a matter of significant public concern, bearing directly on the coherence of Western policy toward Ukraine and the relationship between military strategy and energy market stability. This request is not made for commercial purposes. If a full waiver is not granted, I request that fees be limited to duplication costs only, and that I be notified before any fees exceeding $25.00 are incurred.

EXPEDITED PROCESSING REQUEST

I request expedited processing pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E) on the grounds of urgency to inform the public concerning actual or alleged federal government activity. Ukraine's campaign of long-range strikes on Russian energy infrastructure is an ongoing and consequential dimension of the war with direct implications for global energy markets and U.S. foreign policy. Timely disclosure will contribute directly to informed public debate about U.S. policy decisions in this area.

FORM OF PRODUCTION

Please produce all responsive records in electronic format (PDF or searchable text) via email or electronic delivery. If any records are withheld in whole or in part, I request a Vaughn index identifying each withheld record, the exemption(s) claimed, and a particularized explanation of how the exemption applies. I also request that all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions of withheld records be released.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Tim McDonnell

title: FOIA Request — EIA and DOE Analysis: Impact of Ukrainian Drone Strikes on Russian Oil Refining Capacity and Global Refined Product Markets (January 1, 2024 – December 31, 2025)

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: FOIA Request — EIA and DOE Analysis: Impact of Ukrainian Drone Strikes on Russian Oil Refining Capacity and Global Refined Product Markets (January 1, 2024 – December 31, 2025)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I hereby request copies of all records in the possession of the Department of Energy — including the Energy Information Administration (EIA), the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management (FECM), and the Office of the Secretary — related to analytical assessments of the impact of Ukrainian long-range drone strikes on Russian oil refining capacity and the effect of those strikes on global refined petroleum product markets. The requested time period is January 1, 2024 through December 31, 2025.

BACKGROUND

Beginning in late 2023 and significantly intensifying in early 2024, Ukraine conducted a sustained campaign of drone strikes against Russian oil refineries and export infrastructure. By mid-2024, publicly available analyses indicated that a substantial share of Russian refining capacity had been affected. These strikes raised questions about their effect on global diesel, gasoline, and jet fuel markets, on Russian crude export volumes, and on the functioning of the Russian oil price cap mechanism. The EIA's ongoing monitoring of global oil and refined product markets makes it a primary repository for unclassified analytical records on these questions.

RECORDS SOUGHT

All EIA internal reports, briefing documents, working papers, or analyses assessing the impact of Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil refining capacity, including estimates of refining capacity taken offline, production losses at specific facilities, and the aggregate effect on Russia's refined product output;
EIA assessments of the effect of reduced Russian refining capacity on global diesel, gasoline, naphtha, and jet fuel markets, including any analyses of price effects, trade flow adjustments, and alternative supply responses;
Any EIA analyses of the relationship between Ukrainian strikes on Russian refineries and Russia's crude oil export volumes — specifically any assessments of whether damaged domestic refining capacity led to increased crude exports, and what effect that had on global crude markets and the price cap;
EIA Special Reports, Short-Term Energy Outlook supplementary analyses, or internal briefing materials prepared for DOE leadership or White House officials that addressed Russian energy infrastructure damage from Ukrainian strikes;
Communications between EIA analysts and DOE policy staff — including the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management and the Office of the Secretary — in which the refinery strike campaign and its market implications were discussed;
Any DOE or EIA communications with the International Energy Agency (IEA), the U.S. Energy Information Administration's international counterparts, or allied energy agencies in which the market impact of Ukrainian strikes on Russian refining infrastructure was assessed or discussed;
Records of any formal or informal requests from the National Security Council, National Economic Council, State Department, or Treasury Department for EIA analysis related to the Ukrainian oil infrastructure strike campaign, and the responses to those requests.

NOTE ON CLASSIFICATION

EIA's market analysis and modeling work is overwhelmingly unclassified in nature. I expect the substantial majority of records responsive to this request to be producible without redaction. To the extent any records contain embedded classified annexes or references to intelligence sources, I ask that those portions be redacted and the remainder produced. I also note that any EIA analysis prepared for public release — such as contributions to the Short-Term Energy Outlook or the Annual Energy Outlook — should be produced in full along with any underlying working papers or internal drafts.

KEY OFFICIALS

Relevant officials include: EIA Administrator, the EIA's international energy analysis staff, the Energy Secretary, and relevant staff in the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management. I ask that records custodians search email accounts, shared analytical workspaces, and relevant document repositories for the requested period.
FEE WAIVER REQUEST

I request a waiver of all fees pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii). I am a journalist engaged in research for a book on U.S. energy policy and the geopolitical dimensions of the Russia-Ukraine war. EIA's analytical records on the market impact of the Ukrainian refinery strike campaign are precisely the kind of unclassified government analysis that FOIA is designed to make available to the public. Disclosure will directly inform public understanding of a consequential and underreported dimension of the war's economic effects. This request is not made for commercial purposes. If a full waiver is not granted, I request that fees be limited to duplication costs only, and that I be notified before any fees exceeding $25.00 are incurred.

EXPEDITED PROCESSING REQUEST

I request expedited processing pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E) on the grounds of urgency to inform the public concerning actual or alleged federal government activity. Ukraine's campaign of long-range strikes on Russian energy infrastructure is an ongoing and consequential dimension of the war with direct implications for global energy markets and U.S. foreign policy. Timely disclosure will contribute directly to informed public debate about U.S. policy decisions in this area.

FORM OF PRODUCTION

Please produce all responsive records in electronic format (PDF or searchable text) via email or electronic delivery. If any records are withheld in whole or in part, I request a Vaughn index identifying each withheld record, the exemption(s) claimed, and a particularized explanation of how the exemption applies. I also request that all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions of withheld records be released.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Tim McDonnell

title: FOIA Request — Unclassified Congressional Notifications, Cover Letters, and Summaries: Ukrainian Long-Range Strikes on Russian Energy Infrastructure (January 1, 2024 – December 31, 2024) (Department of Defense, Office of the Secretary of Defense)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I hereby request copies of all unclassified records related to congressional notifications, cover letters, transmittal letters, or unclassified summaries provided by the Department of Defense — or any component thereof — to the United States Congress in connection with Ukrainian long-range drone strikes on Russian oil refining and energy export infrastructure. The requested time period is January 1, 2024 through December 31, 2025.

BACKGROUND AND LEGAL BASIS

The Department of Defense is subject to several statutory reporting and notification obligations to Congress, including under the Arms Export Control Act, the Foreign Assistance Act, the National Security Act, and various annual National Defense Authorization Acts. Notifications made pursuant to these authorities — including those related to security assistance to Ukraine, assessments of Ukrainian military capabilities and operations, or assessments of the impact of those operations on regional stability — may include both classified and unclassified components. This request is limited to the unclassified components: cover letters, transmittal documents, unclassified summaries, and any portions of notifications that are not classified.

RECORDS SOUGHT

All unclassified cover letters, transmittal letters, and unclassified summaries or abstracts accompanying any congressional notification provided by the Office of the Secretary of Defense, the Joint Chiefs of Staff, or any defense agency related to Ukrainian military operations against Russian energy, oil, or refining infrastructure;
Any unclassified portions of reports or notifications provided to the House and Senate Armed Services Committees, the House and Senate Foreign Relations/Foreign Affairs Committees, or the House and Senate Intelligence Committees related to Ukrainian strikes on Russian energy infrastructure or the U.S. assessment of the impact of such strikes;
Unclassified portions of any DOD assessments provided to Congress regarding the effectiveness of Ukrainian long-range drone capabilities against Russian energy infrastructure, including any unclassified summary findings;
Any unclassified records reflecting DOD's assessment of the economic or strategic impact of Ukrainian oil infrastructure strikes on Russia's ability to fund or sustain its military operations;
Unclassified portions of any DOD records reflecting interagency deliberations — including with the State Department, NSC, or Treasury — regarding U.S. policy toward Ukrainian strikes on Russian energy infrastructure, to the extent those deliberations were reflected in congressional notifications or related documents;
Any unclassified after-action assessments, lessons-learned documents, or public-facing briefing materials produced by DOD components regarding the Ukrainian oil infrastructure strike campaign.

SCOPE CLARIFICATION

I understand that the underlying classified congressional notifications are not subject to FOIA disclosure. This request is expressly limited to: (1) unclassified cover letters and transmittal documents accompanying classified notifications; (2) unclassified summaries or abstracts that may accompany or be appended to classified reports; and (3) any entirely unclassified notifications, assessments, or briefing materials related to this subject. I ask that DOD apply the principle of maximum segregability and produce all portions of responsive documents that do not themselves contain classified information, even where the parent document is classified.

KEY OFFICIALS AND OFFICES

Relevant offices include: the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense for Policy, the Office of the Assistant Secretary of Defense for International Security Affairs (Europe and NATO), the Joint Staff (J5 Strategic Plans and Policy), and the Defense Intelligence Agency's energy and economic analysis components. I ask that records custodians search relevant document repositories, congressional correspondence tracking systems, and legislative affairs offices for the requested period.

FEE WAIVER REQUEST

I request a waiver of all fees pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii). I am a journalist engaged in research for a book on U.S. energy policy and the geopolitical dimensions of the Russia-Ukraine war. The unclassified components of congressional notifications regarding Ukrainian military operations are precisely the category of records designed to maintain public accountability over executive branch activities. Disclosure will contribute significantly to public understanding of U.S. policy deliberations on a consequential dimension of the conflict. This request is not made for commercial purposes. If a full waiver is not granted, I request that fees be limited to duplication costs only, and that I be notified before any fees exceeding $25.00 are incurred.

EXPEDITED PROCESSING REQUEST

I request expedited processing pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E). Ukraine's oil infrastructure strike campaign is an ongoing and evolving military and economic story with direct implications for U.S. policy and global energy markets. Timely disclosure of the unclassified record will contribute directly to informed public debate.

FORM OF PRODUCTION

Please produce all responsive records in electronic format (PDF or searchable text) via email or electronic delivery. If any records are withheld in whole or in part, I request a Vaughn index identifying each withheld record, the exemption(s) claimed, and a particularized explanation of how the exemption applies. I also request that all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions of withheld records be released.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Tim McDonnell
